title: Compute mirror of a binary tree
description: Convert a binary tree into its mirror image (swap left and right subtrees recursively)

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2, right: 3)
examples.explanation: Step 1: Start at root 1. Recursively mirror left subtree (node 2). Step 2: At node 2, it has no children, so mirror(2) returns 2 after swapping null children (no change). Step 3: Recursively mirror right subtree (node 3). At node 3, no children, mirror(3) returns 3. Step 4: Back at node 1, swap left and right pointers: left becomes 3, right becomes 2. Return node 1.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4, right: 3 with right: 5)
examples.output: Tree: 1 (left: 3 with left: 5, right: 2 with right: 4)
examples.explanation: Step 1: At root 1, mirror left subtree (node 2). Step 2: At node 2, mirror its left (node 4): node 4 has no children, returns 4. Mirror right (null) returns null. Swap at node 2: left becomes null, right becomes 4. Return node 2. Step 3: Mirror right subtree (node 3): mirror left (null) returns null, mirror right (node 5) returns 5. Swap at node 3: left becomes 5, right becomes null. Return node 3. Step 4: Back at root 1, swap left and right: left becomes node 3, right becomes node 2. Return node 1.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive Post-Order Traversal
approaches.summary: Traverse the tree recursively using post-order (left, right, root). At each node, swap its left and right subtrees after processing both children. This naturally handles the mirror transformation from bottom-up.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is tree height (worst O(n) for skewed tree)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Mirror Binary Tree via Recursive Post-Order Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution to mirror a binary tree by recursively swapping left and right subtrees in post-order fashion.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that to mirror an entire binary tree, we need to mirror each subtree from the bottom up. By using post-order traversal (left, right, root), we ensure that both children of a node are already mirrored before we swap them at the current node. This bottom-up transformation guarantees that the entire tree gets mirrored correctly. The algorithm starts at the root: if the node is null, return null (base case). Otherwise, recursively mirror the left subtree, then recursively mirror the right subtree. After both subtrees are mirrored, swap the left and right child pointers of the current node. Finally, return the node. This process propagates up the tree, with each node receiving mirrored subtrees from below and then swapping them. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node exactly once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is the tree height due to the recursion stack; worst-case O(n) for a skewed tree. This connects to L1 as it implements the complete recursive function. Common mistakes include swapping before recursing (which would use original subtrees) or forgetting the base case. Real code pattern: def mirror(node): if not node: return None; left_mirrored = mirror(node.left); right_mirrored = mirror(node.right); node.left, node.right = right_mirrored, left_mirrored; return node.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2, right: 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Recursively mirror left subtree (node 2). Step 2: At node 2, it has no children, so mirror(2) returns 2 after swapping null children (no change). Step 3: Recursively mirror right subtree (node 3). At node 3, no children, mirror(3) returns 3. Step 4: Back at node 1, swap left and right pointers: left becomes 3, right becomes 2. Return node 1.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4, right: 3 with right: 5)
examples.output: Tree: 1 (left: 3 with left: 5, right: 2 with right: 4)
examples.trace: Step 1: At root 1, mirror left subtree (node 2). Step 2: At node 2, mirror its left (node 4): node 4 has no children, returns 4. Mirror right (null) returns null. Swap at node 2: left becomes null, right becomes 4. Return node 2. Step 3: Mirror right subtree (node 3): mirror left (null) returns null, mirror right (node 5) returns 5. Swap at node 3: left becomes 5, right becomes null. Return node 3. Step 4: Back at root 1, swap left and right: left becomes node 3, right becomes node 2. Return node 1.

testCases.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4, right: 3 with right: 5)
testCases.expected: Tree: 1 (left: 3 with left: 5, right: 2 with right: 4)

testCases.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4 and right: 5, right: 3 with left: 6 and right: 7)
testCases.expected: Tree: 1 (left: 3 with left: 7 and right: 6, right: 2 with left: 5 and right: 4)

testCases.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4 and right: 5, right: 3 with left: 6))
testCases.expected: Tree: 1 (left: 3 with right: 6, right: 2 with left: 5 and right: 4)

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Recursive Mirror Function for a Single Node
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that mirrors the subtree rooted at a given node.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the full solution: a function that takes a tree node and returns its mirrored version. The intuition is that mirroring a node involves mirroring its entire subtree recursively. The algorithm: if the node is null, return null (base case). Otherwise, recursively call the function on the left child to get the mirrored left subtree, and on the right child to get the mirrored right subtree. Then, swap the left and right pointers of the current node: set left to the mirrored right subtree and right to the mirrored left subtree. Finally, return the node. This ensures that after the call, the node's subtree is fully mirrored. Time complexity for this function on a subtree of size m is O(m), as it visits each node in that subtree once. Space complexity is O(height of subtree) for recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by being the core recursive function called from the root. Common mistakes include not handling null nodes or swapping pointers incorrectly. Real code pattern: def mirror_node(node): if node is None: return None; left_mirror = mirror_node(node.left); right_mirror = mirror_node(node.right); node.left = right_mirror; node.right = left_mirror; return node.

examples.input: Node: 2 with left: 4, right: null
examples.trace: Step 1: Node 2 is not null. Recursively mirror left child (node 4). Step 2: At node 4, it has no children, so mirror_node(4) returns 4 after swapping nulls. Step 3: Recursively mirror right child (null), returns null. Step 4: Swap pointers at node 2: left becomes null, right becomes node 4. Return node 2.

examples.input: Node: 3 with left: null, right: 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Node 3 not null. Mirror left child (null) returns null. Step 2: Mirror right child (node 5): node 5 has no children, returns 5. Step 3: Swap at node 3: left becomes node 5, right becomes null. Return node 3.

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left: 2 with left: 4, right: null
testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: null, right: 2 with right: 4

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left: null, right: 2 with right: 3
testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: 2 with left: 3, right: null

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left: 2, right: 3 with left: 4
testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: 3 with left: null and right: 4, right: 2

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left: 2 with left: 4 and right: 5, right: null
testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: null, right: 2 with left: 5 and right: 4

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left: null, right: 2 with left: 3 and right: 4
testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: 2 with left: 4 and right: 3, right: null

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left: 2 with left: 3, right: 4 with right: 5
testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: 4 with left: 5 and right: null, right: 2 with left: null and right: 3

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left: 2 with left: 3 with left: 4, right: null
testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: null, right: 2 with right: 3 with right: 4

ladders.title: Core Logic — Post-Order Traversal with Pointer Swapping
ladders.desc: Execute post-order traversal (left, right, root) and swap left/right pointers at each node.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central technique: traversing a binary tree in post-order and swapping child pointers at each node. The intuition is that post-order ensures we process children before their parent, which is crucial for mirroring because we need both subtrees to be mirrored before we swap them at the parent. The algorithm: for a given node, first recursively traverse the left subtree, then recursively traverse the right subtree. After both traversals complete, swap the left and right pointers of the current node. This swap operation is what creates the mirror effect. The traversal itself doesn't return values but modifies the tree in place. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion. This is the core logic used in L1's function. Common mistakes include using pre-order or in-order traversal, which would swap before children are mirrored. Real code pattern: def post_order_swap(node): if not node: return; post_order_swap(node.left); post_order_swap(node.right); node.left, node.right = node.right, node.left.

examples.input: Node: 1 with left: 2, right: 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 1. Go left to node 2. Step 2: At node 2, go left (null), return. Go right (null), return. Swap at node 2: left and right are null, so no change. Step 3: Back to node 1, go right to node 3. At node 3, left null, right null, swap (no change). Step 4: Back to node 1, swap: left becomes node 3, right becomes node 2.

examples.input: Node: 1 with left: 2 with left: 4, right: null
examples.output: Node: 1 with left: null, right: 2 with right: 4
examples.trace: Step 1: At node 1, go left to node 2. Step 2: At node 2, go left to node 4. At node 4, left null, right null, swap (no change). Step 3: Back to node 2, go right (null), return. Swap at node 2: left becomes null, right becomes node 4. Step 4: Back to node 1, go right (null), return. Swap at node 1: left becomes null, right becomes node 2.

testCases.expected: Node: 1 (left: 3 with left: null and right: 4, right: 2)

testCases.input: Node: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4 and right: 5, right: null)
testCases.expected: Node: 1 (left: null, right: 2 with left: 5 and right: 4)

testCases.input: Node: 1 (left: null, right: 2 with left: 3 and right: 4)
testCases.expected: Node: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4 and right: 3, right: null)

testCases.input: Node: 1 (left: 2 with left: 3, right: 4 with right: 5)
testCases.expected: Node: 1 (left: 4 with left: 5 and right: null, right: 2 with left: null and right: 3)

testCases.input: Node: 1 (left: 2 with left: 3 with left: 4, right: null)
testCases.expected: Node: 1 (left: null, right: 2 with right: 3 with right: 4)

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal (Post-Order)
ladders.desc: Traverse a binary tree recursively in post-order: left subtree, right subtree, then current node.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is about traversing a binary tree using post-order recursion, which is essential for the mirroring approach. The intuition is that post-order visits nodes after their children, making it ideal for operations that depend on processed subtrees. The algorithm: define a function that takes a node. If the node is null, return (base case). Otherwise, recursively call the function on the left child, then recursively call on the right child, and finally process the current node (e.g., print its value or perform an action). This order ensures that for any node, its left and right subtrees are fully traversed before the node itself. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(h) due to recursion stack. This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal framework onto which swapping is added. Common mistakes include infinite recursion by missing base case or confusing traversal orders. Real code pattern: def post_order(node): if not node: return; post_order(node.left); post_order(node.right); print(node.val).

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2, right: 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Go left to node 2. Step 2: At node 2, left null, return. Right null, return. Process node 2 (print 2). Step 3: Back to node 1, go right to node 3. At node 3, left null, right null, process node 3 (print 3). Step 4: Back to node 1, process node 1 (print 1). Order: 2, 3, 1.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4, right: null)
examples.trace: Step 1: At root 1, go left to node 2. Step 2: At node 2, go left to node 4. At node 4, left null, right null, process 4. Step 3: Back to node 2, go right (null), return. Process node 2. Step 4: Back to node 1, go right (null), return. Process node 1. Order: 4, 2, 1.

testCases.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4 and right: 5, right: null)
Tree: 1 (left: null, right: 2 with left: 3 and right: 4)
Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 3, right: 4 with right: 5)
Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 3 with left: 4, right: null)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swapping Left and Right Child Pointers
ladders.desc: Swap the left and right child pointers of a binary tree node.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves exchanging the references to the left and right children of a single binary tree node. The intuition is that mirroring a tree requires reversing the left-right orientation at each node, which is achieved by swapping these pointers. The algorithm: given a node, if it is not null, temporarily store its left child in a variable, set the left child to the right child, and set the right child to the stored left child. This operation is performed in constant time O(1) and uses O(1) extra space. It's a basic building block used in L2 and L3 for the swap step during traversal. Common mistakes include swapping values instead of pointers or not handling null children correctly. Real code pattern: temp = node.left; node.left = node.right; node.right = temp; or in Python: node.left, node.right = node.right, node.left.

examples.input: Node: 1 with left: 2, right: 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Node 1 has left child 2 and right child 3. Step 2: Swap: set left to right (3), set right to original left (2). Result: left is 3, right is 2.

examples.input: Node: 4 with left: null, right: 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Node 4 has left null and right 5. Step 2: Swap: left becomes 5, right becomes null.

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left: null, right: null
testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: null, right: null

testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: 3, right: 2 with left: 4

testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: 3 with right: 5, right: 2

testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: 3, right: 2 with left: 4 and right: 5

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left: 2, right: 3 with left: 6 and right: 7
testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left: 3 with left: 6 and right: 7, right: 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Null Handling
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a binary tree node and how to handle null (empty) nodes.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a binary tree node typically has a value and two pointers/references to left and right children. In many implementations, a node is defined as a class or struct with attributes: val (the data), left (pointer to left child), and right (pointer to right child). A null (or None) represents an empty subtree or leaf node with no children. The intuition is that binary trees are recursive structures where each node can have up to two children, and null indicates the absence of a child. Handling null is crucial in algorithms to avoid errors: when traversing, if a node is null, we return or skip it (base case). This foundation is used in all higher levels for recursion and pointer operations. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. Common mistakes include accessing attributes of null nodes or not checking for null before operations. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. To handle null: if node is None: return.

examples.input: Node with val: 5, left: null, right: null
examples.output: A leaf node with value 5 and no children.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create a node with value 5. Step 2: Set left pointer to null (meaning no left child). Step 3: Set right pointer to null (no right child). This represents a single node in the tree.

examples.input: Node with val: 1, left: node with val 2, right: null
examples.output: A node with value 1, left child 2, and no right child.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node for value 2 with left null, right null. Step 2: Create node for value 1. Step 3: Set left pointer of node 1 to node 2. Step 4: Set right pointer of node 1 to null.

testCases.expected: Represents an empty tree or missing child.
Node with value 10, left child 5, no right child.
Node with value 10, no left child, right child 15.
Node with value 10, left child 5, right child 15.

testCases.input: Node: val=10, left=Node(val=5 with left=Node(val=2)), right=null
testCases.expected: Node 10 with left child 5, which has left child 2; no right child.

testCases.input: Node: val=10, left=null, right=Node(val=15 with right=Node(val=20))
testCases.expected: Node 10 with no left child, right child 15 which has right child 20.

testCases.expected: Node 10 with left child 5, right child 15 which has left child 12.

testCases.expected: Node 10 with left child 5 (which has children 2 and 7), right child 15.

testCases.input: Node: val=10, left=Node(val=5 with left=Node(val=2 with left=Node(val=1))), right=null
testCases.expected: Node 10 with left child 5, which has left child 2, which has left child 1; no right child.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Recursive Pre-Order Traversal
approaches.summary: Traverse the tree recursively using pre-order (root, left, right). At each node, immediately swap left and right subtrees before recursively processing the new children. This works top-down.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is tree height (worst O(n) for skewed tree)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Mirror Binary Tree via Recursive Pre-Order Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution to convert a binary tree into its mirror image using recursive pre-order traversal.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires transforming a binary tree into its mirror image, where left and right subtrees are swapped at every node. The recursive pre-order traversal approach works top-down: at each node, we first swap its left and right children, then recursively process the new left (originally right) and new right (originally left) subtrees. Intuitively, this mirrors the tree by flipping each node's children before descending, ensuring the entire structure is inverted. The algorithm starts at the root, swaps children, then calls itself on the new left and right. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once, and space complexity is O(h) due to recursion stack, where h is the tree height (worst-case O(n) for skewed trees). This connects to L1 by implementing the full mirroring process using the swap-and-recurse subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling null nodes (base case), swapping after recursion (which would not work top-down), or modifying the tree incorrectly. Real code pattern: define a function mirror(node): if node is null, return; swap node.left and node.right; mirror(node.left); mirror(node.right).

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2, right: 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Swap left=2 and right=3 → now left=3, right=2. Step 2: Recurse on new left=3 (original right). Node 3 has no children, base case returns. Step 3: Recurse on new right=2 (original left). Node 2 has no children, base case returns. Done.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left:4, right:5, right: 3 with left:6, right:7)
examples.output: Mirrored: 1 (left: 3 with left:7, right:6, right: 2 with left:5, right:4)
examples.trace: Step 1: Root 1: swap left=2 and right=3 → left=3, right=2. Step 2: Recurse on new left=3. Node 3: swap left=6 and right=7 → left=7, right=6. Recurse on 7 (null), 6 (null). Step 3: Recurse on new right=2. Node 2: swap left=4 and right=5 → left=5, right=4. Recurse on 5 (null), 4 (null). Done.

testCases.input: 1 (left: 2 with left:4, right:5, right: 3 with left:6, right:7)
testCases.expected: 1 (left: 3 with left:7, right:6, right: 2 with left:5, right:4)

testCases.input: 1 (left: 2 with left:4 with left:8, right: null, right:5, right: 3)
testCases.expected: 1 (left: 3, right: 2 with left:5, right:4 with left:null, right:8)

testCases.input: Full tree: 1 (left: 2 with left:4, right:5, right: 3 with left:6, right:7)
testCases.expected: 1 (left: 3 with left:7, right:6, right: 2 with left:5, right:4)

testCases.input: Complex: 1 (left: 2 with left:4, right:5 with left:10, right: null, right: 3 with left:6, right:7 with left: null, right:11)
testCases.expected: 1 (left: 3 with left:7 with left:11, right: null, right:6, right: 2 with left:5 with left:null, right:10, right:4)

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Swap and Recurse on Binary Tree Node
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that swaps children and recurses on a binary tree node.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the mirroring algorithm. For a given node, it swaps its left and right children, then recursively calls itself on the new children. Intuition: by swapping first, we ensure the tree is mirrored top-down; recursing on the swapped children processes the entire subtree correctly. Step-by-step: check if node is null (base case), swap left and right pointers, call function on node.left, call function on node.right. Time: O(1) per node plus recursion, overall O(n). Space: O(h) for recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by being the core operation applied to each node. Common mistakes: forgetting base case (causes null pointer errors), swapping after recursion (does not mirror properly), or not updating pointers correctly. Real code: def mirror_node(node): if not node: return; node.left, node.right = node.right, node.left; mirror_node(node.left); mirror_node(node.right).

examples.input: Node: A (left: B, right: C)
examples.trace: Step 1: Node A not null. Step 2: Swap left=B and right=C → left=C, right=B. Step 3: Recurse on left=C (base case if null). Step 4: Recurse on right=B (base case if null). Done.

examples.input: Node: X (left: Y with left:Z, right: null, right: W)
examples.output: After: X (left: W, right: Y with left:null, right:Z)
examples.trace: Step 1: Node X not null. Swap left=Y and right=W → left=W, right=Y. Step 2: Recurse on left=W (base case). Step 3: Recurse on right=Y. Node Y: swap left=Z and right=null → left=null, right=Z. Recurse on null, Z. Done.

testCases.input: Node: 7 (left: 14, right: 21 with left:42, right: null)
Node: 50 (left: 25, right: 75 with left: null, right:100)
Node: 0 (left: 1 with left:2, right:3, right: 4 with left:5, right:6)
testCases.expected: 0 (left: 4 with left:6, right:5, right: 1 with left:3, right:2)

testCases.input: Node: 8 (left: 16 with left:32, right:64, right: 24 with left:48, right:96)
testCases.expected: 8 (left: 24 with left:96, right:48, right: 16 with left:64, right:32)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pre-Order Traversal with Immediate Swap
ladders.desc: Understand and apply pre-order traversal (root, left, right) while swapping children at the root step.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the traversal pattern: pre-order visits the root first, then left subtree, then right subtree. For mirroring, we modify this by swapping left and right children immediately after visiting the root, before recursing. Intuition: swapping early ensures the recursion processes the mirrored structure. Step-by-step: at a node, if null, return; swap left and right; recurse on new left; recurse on new right. Time: O(n) as each node visited once. Space: O(h) for recursion. This connects to L1 as the algorithmic flow. Common mistakes: using in-order or post-order (which would not mirror correctly), or swapping after recursion. Real code: def pre_order_swap(node): if not node: return; swap(node.left, node.right); pre_order_swap(node.left); pre_order_swap(node.right).

examples.input: Tree: R (left: A, right: B)
examples.output: Traversal with swap: visit R, swap A and B, recurse on new left=B, new right=A
examples.trace: Step 1: At R, not null. Swap left=A and right=B → left=B, right=A. Step 2: Recurse on left=B (if B has children, process similarly). Step 3: Recurse on right=A. Done.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left:4, right: null, right: 3)
examples.output: Traversal: visit 1, swap 2 and 3, recurse on new left=3, new right=2
examples.trace: Step 1: At 1, swap left=2 and right=3 → left=3, right=2. Step 2: Recurse on left=3 (null children, base). Step 3: Recurse on right=2. At 2, swap left=4 and right=null → left=null, right=4. Recurse on null, 4. Done.

testCases.expected: Visit X, swap nulls, recurse nulls
Visit P, swap Q and R, recurse on R, Q
Visit 10, swap 20 and null, recurse on null, 20; at 20, swap 40 and null, recurse on null, 40
Visit 5, swap null and 15, recurse on 15, null

testCases.input: Node: 30 (left: 60, right: 90 with left:180, right: null)
testCases.expected: Visit 30, swap 60 and 90, recurse on 90, 60; at 90, swap 180 and null, recurse on null, 180
Visit 2, swap 4 and 6, recurse on 6, 4; at 4, swap 8 and 16, recurse on 16, 8

testCases.input: Node: 9 (left: 18, right: 27 with left: null, right:54)
testCases.expected: Visit 9, swap 18 and 27, recurse on 27, 18; at 27, swap null and 54, recurse on 54, null

testCases.input: Node: 1 (left: 3 with left:7, right:15, right: 5 with left:11, right:23)
testCases.expected: Visit 1, swap 3 and 5, recurse on 5, 3; at 5, swap 11 and 23, recurse on 23, 11; at 3, swap 7 and 15, recurse on 15, 7

testCases.input: Node: 50 (left: 25 with left:12, right:37, right: 75 with left:62, right:87)
testCases.expected: Visit 50, swap 25 and 75, recurse on 75, 25; at 75, swap 62 and 87, recurse on 87, 62; at 25, swap 12 and 37, recurse on 37, 12

ladders.title: Building Block — Swapping Pointers in a Binary Tree Node
ladders.desc: Learn to swap the left and right child pointers of a binary tree node.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves manipulating a node's structure by exchanging its left and right references. Intuition: swapping pointers changes the tree's connectivity, which is essential for mirroring. Step-by-step: given a node, store its left child in a temp variable, assign left to right, assign right to temp. Alternatively, use simultaneous assignment. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This feeds into L2 as the action performed during traversal. Common mistakes: losing references by not using temp, swapping values instead of pointers (incorrect for mirroring), or not handling null pointers properly. Real code: temp = node.left; node.left = node.right; node.right = temp; or node.left, node.right = node.right, node.left.

examples.input: Node: N (left: L, right: R)
examples.output: After swap: N (left: R, right: L)
examples.trace: Step 1: Node N has left=L, right=R. Step 2: temp = L. Step 3: node.left = R. Step 4: node.right = temp = L. Done.

examples.input: Node: X (left: null, right: Y)
examples.output: After swap: X (left: Y, right: null)
examples.trace: Step 1: left=null, right=Y. Step 2: temp = null. Step 3: node.left = Y. Step 4: node.right = null. Done.

testCases.input: Node: I (left: J with left:K, right: L)
testCases.expected: I (left: L, right: J with left:K, right: L) [Note: J's structure unchanged, only I's pointers swapped]

testCases.input: Node: M (left: N, right: O with left:P, right:Q)
testCases.expected: M (left: O with left:P, right:Q, right: N)

testCases.input: Node: R (left: S with left:T, right:U, right: V)
testCases.expected: R (left: V, right: S with left:T, right:U)

testCases.input: Node: W (left: X, right: Y with left: null, right:Z)
testCases.expected: W (left: Y with left: null, right:Z, right: X)

testCases.input: Node: Alpha (left: Beta, right: Gamma with left:Delta, right: Epsilon)
testCases.expected: Alpha (left: Gamma with left:Delta, right: Epsilon, right: Beta)

testCases.input: Node: Root (left: LeftChild, right: RightChild with left:LeftGrand, right:RightGrand)
testCases.expected: Root (left: RightChild with left:LeftGrand, right:RightGrand, right: LeftChild)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Modifying Binary Tree Node Children
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation to get and set the left and right child pointers of a node.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves interacting with a binary tree node's structure. A node typically has attributes like 'left' and 'right' that reference its children. Intuition: to manipulate a tree, we need to read and update these pointers. Step-by-step: to access left child, use node.left; to access right child, use node.right; to modify, assign new values like node.left = some_node. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This connects to L3 as the prerequisite for swapping. Common mistakes: assuming children exist without null checks, incorrectly referencing attributes, or not understanding pointer semantics. Real code: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Then, left_child = node.left; node.right = new_node.

examples.input: Node: 10 with left=20, right=30; get left, set right to 40
examples.trace: Step 1: Access node.left → returns 20. Step 2: Access node.right → returns 30. Step 3: Set node.right = 40. Now node has left=20, right=40.

examples.input: Node: 5 with left=null, right=15; get right, set left to 25
examples.trace: Step 1: Access node.left → null. Step 2: Access node.right → 15. Step 3: Set node.left = 25. Now node has left=25, right=15.

testCases.input: Node: 1 with left=2, right=3; get left
Node: 4 with left=null, right=5; get right
Node: 6 with left=7, right=null; set left to 8
Node: 9 with left=10, right=11; set right to 12
Node: 13 with left=null, right=null; get left and right
Node: 14 with left=15, right=16; get left and set right to 17
Node: 18 with left=19, right=20; set left to null
Node: 21 with left=22, right=23; get right and set left to 24
Node: 25 with left=26, right=27; set left to 28 and right to 29
Node: 30 with left=31, right=32; get left, get right, set left to right, set right to left

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Null Pointers
ladders.desc: Understand the basic building block of a binary tree: a node with value and child pointers, including null as empty.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces a binary tree node, which is a data structure with a value and two references (left and right) to other nodes or null. Intuition: trees are built from nodes linked together; null represents the absence of a child, crucial for defining tree boundaries. A node is defined with attributes: value (e.g., an integer), left (pointer to left child), right (pointer to right child). Step-by-step: create a node with a value, initialize left and right to null. Time: O(1) to create. Space: O(1) per node. This feeds into L4 as the basis for accessing children. Common mistakes: not initializing pointers to null, confusing nodes with values, or misunderstanding null as a valid state. Real code: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Example: node = TreeNode(5) creates a node with value 5 and null children.

examples.input: Create a node with value 10
examples.trace: Step 1: Define node with val=10. Step 2: Set left = None. Step 3: Set right = None. Result is a single node.

examples.input: Node with value 3, left child with value 6, right child null
examples.output: Node: val=3, left points to node(6), right=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node A with val=3, left=null, right=null. Step 2: Create node B with val=6, left=null, right=null. Step 3: Set A.left = B. Now A has left pointing to B, right null.

testCases.expected: val=3, left points to node(4), right=null
val=5, left=null, right points to node(6)

testCases.input: Node val=7, left child val=8, right child val=9
testCases.expected: val=7, left points to node(8), right points to node(9)

testCases.input: Node val=11, left child null, right child val=12
testCases.expected: val=11, left=null, right points to node(12)

testCases.input: Node val=13, left child val=14, right child null
testCases.expected: val=13, left points to node(14), right=null

testCases.input: Node val=15, left child val=16 with left=null, right=null
testCases.expected: val=15, left points to node(16 with left=null, right=null), right=null

testCases.expected: val=17, left points to node(18 with left=null, right=null), right points to node(19 with left=null, right=null)

approaches.name: Approach 3: Iterative BFS Using Queue
approaches.summary: Use breadth-first search with a queue to process nodes level by level. At each node, swap its left and right children, then enqueue them for further processing. This avoids recursion overhead.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is maximum width of tree (worst O(n))

ladders.title: Full Problem — Mirror Binary Tree Iteratively Using BFS Queue
ladders.desc: Complete iterative solution using BFS queue to mirror a binary tree by swapping left/right children at each node.
ladders.explanation: This approach mirrors a binary tree using an iterative breadth-first search (BFS) with a queue. The intuition is to process nodes level by level, swapping left and right children at each node, then enqueueing those children for further processing. This avoids recursion overhead and uses O(n) time and O(w) space where w is the maximum width of the tree. The algorithm starts by checking if the root is null, returning immediately if so. Otherwise, it initializes a queue with the root. While the queue is not empty, it dequeues a node, swaps its left and right children, and if those children are not null, enqueues them. This ensures all nodes are processed exactly once. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. If root is null, return null. 2. Create a queue and enqueue root. 3. While queue is not empty: a. Dequeue current node. b. Swap left and right children of current node. c. If left child is not null, enqueue it. d. If right child is not null, enqueue it. 4. Return root. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(w) where w is the maximum number of nodes at any level (worst-case O(n) for a skewed tree). This connects to the full problem by providing a complete, efficient solution. Common mistakes include not handling null roots, forgetting to enqueue children after swapping, or using a stack instead of a queue (which would be DFS). Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def mirror_tree(root): if not root: return None queue = [root] while queue: node = queue.pop(0) node.left, node.right = node.right, node.left if node.left: queue.append(node.left) if node.right: queue.append(node.right) return root.

examples.input: Binary tree: 1 (left: 2, right: 3) where 2 has left: 4, right: 5 and 3 has left: 6, right: 7
examples.output: Mirrored tree: 1 (left: 3, right: 2) where 3 has left: 7, right: 6 and 2 has left: 5, right: 4
examples.trace: Step 1: Root is 1, queue = [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, swap left=2 and right=3 to get left=3, right=2. Enqueue 3 and 2. Queue = [3,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 3, swap left=6 and right=7 to get left=7, right=6. Enqueue 7 and 6. Queue = [2,7,6]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, swap left=4 and right=5 to get left=5, right=4. Enqueue 5 and 4. Queue = [7,6,5,4]. Step 5: Dequeue 7, swap left=null and right=null (no change). Queue = [6,5,4]. Step 6: Dequeue 6, swap left=null and right=null. Queue = [5,4]. Step 7: Dequeue 5, swap left=null and right=null. Queue = [4]. Step 8: Dequeue 4, swap left=null and right=null. Queue empty. Done.

examples.input: Binary tree: 10 (left: 5, right: 15) where 5 has left: 2, right: 7 and 15 has left: 12, right: 20
examples.output: Mirrored tree: 10 (left: 15, right: 5) where 15 has left: 20, right: 12 and 5 has left: 7, right: 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Root is 10, queue = [10]. Step 2: Dequeue 10, swap left=5 and right=15 to get left=15, right=5. Enqueue 15 and 5. Queue = [15,5]. Step 3: Dequeue 15, swap left=12 and right=20 to get left=20, right=12. Enqueue 20 and 12. Queue = [5,20,12]. Step 4: Dequeue 5, swap left=2 and right=7 to get left=7, right=2. Enqueue 7 and 2. Queue = [20,12,7,2]. Step 5: Dequeue 20, swap left=null and right=null. Queue = [12,7,2]. Step 6: Dequeue 12, swap left=null and right=null. Queue = [7,2]. Step 7: Dequeue 7, swap left=null and right=null. Queue = [2]. Step 8: Dequeue 2, swap left=null and right=null. Queue empty. Done.

testCases.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 with left: 4, right: 5, right: 3 with left: 6, right: 7)
testCases.expected: 1 (left: 3 with left: 7, right: 6, right: 2 with left: 5, right: 4)

testCases.input: Tree: 10 (left: 5 with left: 3, right: 7, right: 15 with left: 12, right: 18)
testCases.expected: 10 (left: 15 with left: 18, right: 12, right: 5 with left: 7, right: 3)

testCases.input: Tree: 100 (left: 50 with left: 25, right: 75, right: 150 with left: 125, right: 175)
testCases.expected: 100 (left: 150 with left: 175, right: 125, right: 50 with left: 75, right: 25)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Queue Processing with Node Swapping
ladders.desc: Implement the core BFS loop that dequeues nodes, swaps children, and enqueues non-null children.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the iterative BFS loop that processes nodes from a queue to swap their left and right children. The intuition is to traverse the tree level by level using a queue, ensuring each node is visited exactly once, and perform the swap operation at each node. This is the heart of the mirroring algorithm, as it handles the traversal and transformation without recursion. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a queue with the root node. 2. While the queue is not empty: a. Dequeue the front node. b. Swap its left and right children. c. If the left child is not null, enqueue it. d. If the right child is not null, enqueue it. This ensures that after swapping, the children (if any) are added to the queue for further processing, propagating the mirroring down the tree. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes processed, and space complexity is O(w) for the queue. This connects to L0 by being the main loop that drives the full solution. Common mistakes include not enqueueing children after swapping (which would skip subtrees), or enqueueing before swapping (which could lead to incorrect processing order). Real code pattern: def bfs_swap(root): if not root: return queue = [root] while queue: node = queue.pop(0) node.left, node.right = node.right, node.left if node.left: queue.append(node.left) if node.right: queue.append(node.right) return root.

examples.input: Root node with value 1, left=2, right=3. Queue starts with [1].
examples.output: After processing: node 1 swapped to left=3, right=2. Queue becomes [3,2].
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue = [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, swap left=2 and right=3 to left=3, right=2. Enqueue left=3 and right=2. Queue = [3,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 3, swap left=null and right=null (no children). Queue = [2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, swap left=null and right=null. Queue empty. Done.

examples.input: Root node with value 10, left=5 (with left=2, right=7), right=15. Queue starts with [10].
examples.output: After processing: node 10 swapped to left=15, right=5. Queue becomes [15,5].
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue = [10]. Step 2: Dequeue 10, swap left=5 and right=15 to left=15, right=5. Enqueue left=15 and right=5. Queue = [15,5]. Step 3: Dequeue 15, swap left=null and right=null. Queue = [5]. Step 4: Dequeue 5, swap left=2 and right=7 to left=7, right=2. Enqueue left=7 and right=2. Queue = [7,2]. Step 5: Dequeue 7, swap left=null and right=null. Queue = [2]. Step 6: Dequeue 2, swap left=null and right=null. Queue empty. Done.

testCases.expected: 1 (no change, queue becomes empty after processing)
1 swapped to left=null, right=2, queue becomes [2]
1 swapped to left=2, right=null, queue becomes [2]
1 swapped to left=3, right=2, queue becomes [3,2]

testCases.input: Root: 10 with left=5 (children: left=2, right=7), right=15
testCases.expected: 10 swapped to left=15, right=5, queue becomes [15,5]

testCases.input: Root: 100 with left=50, right=150 (both no children)
testCases.expected: 100 swapped to left=150, right=50, queue becomes [150,50]

testCases.input: Root: 3 with left=1, right=4 (left child has left=0, right=2)
testCases.expected: 3 swapped to left=4, right=1, queue becomes [4,1]
8 swapped to left=10, right=3, queue becomes [10,3]

testCases.input: Root: 20 with left=10 (left=5, right=15), right=30 (left=25, right=35)
testCases.expected: 20 swapped to left=30, right=10, queue becomes [30,10]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Swapping Left and Right Children of a Binary Tree Node
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the swap operation that exchanges left and right pointers of a node.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves swapping the left and right children of a binary tree node, which is the fundamental transformation needed to mirror the tree. The intuition is that mirroring a binary tree requires reversing the left-right orientation at each node, and swapping pointers achieves this efficiently. By exchanging the references to the left and right subtrees, the structure is mirrored locally, and when applied recursively or iteratively to all nodes, the entire tree is mirrored. The step-by-step operation: For a given node, if it is not null, temporarily store the left child, assign the right child to the left pointer, and assign the stored left child to the right pointer. This is an O(1) time operation per node. In the context of the full problem, this swap is performed during BFS traversal. Common mistakes include swapping values instead of pointers (which would not mirror the structure if subtrees are complex), or not handling null nodes correctly (swapping null pointers is fine as it just exchanges null references). Real code pattern: def swap_children(node): if node: node.left, node.right = node.right, node.left. This connects to L1 as the key action within the BFS loop.

examples.input: Node with value 1, left=2, right=3
examples.trace: Step 1: Node has left pointer to 2, right pointer to 3. Step 2: Swap: set left to 3, right to 2. Done.

examples.input: Node with value 10, left=5, right=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Node has left pointer to 5, right pointer to null. Step 2: Swap: set left to null, right to 5. Done.

testCases.expected: Node: 1 with left=null, right=null (swap does nothing)

ladders.title: Building Block — BFS Traversal of a Binary Tree Using a Queue
ladders.desc: Implement breadth-first search to visit all nodes level by level without recursion.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves performing a breadth-first search (BFS) traversal on a binary tree using a queue. The intuition is to explore nodes level by level, starting from the root, which is essential for the mirroring algorithm to process each node exactly once in a systematic order. BFS uses a queue to manage the order of node visits: enqueue the root, then repeatedly dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue its non-null children. This ensures that all nodes at the current level are processed before moving to the next level. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. If root is null, return. 2. Create a queue and enqueue root. 3. While queue is not empty: a. Dequeue current node. b. Process node (e.g., print value). c. If left child exists, enqueue it. d. If right child exists, enqueue it. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is enqueued and dequeued once. Space complexity is O(w) for the queue. This connects to L2 as the traversal mechanism that applies the swap operation. Common mistakes include using a stack (which would give DFS), not handling null children, or infinite loops by not dequeuing properly. Real code pattern: def bfs_traversal(root): if not root: return queue = [root] while queue: node = queue.pop(0) print(node.val) if node.left: queue.append(node.left) if node.right: queue.append(node.right).

examples.input: Binary tree: 1 (left: 2, right: 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue = [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, print 1. Enqueue left=2 and right=3. Queue = [2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, print 2. Enqueue left=null and right=null (no enqueue). Queue = [3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, print 3. Enqueue left=null and right=null. Queue empty. Done.

examples.input: Binary tree: 10 (left: 5 with left: 2, right: 7, right: 15)
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue = [10]. Step 2: Dequeue 10, print 10. Enqueue left=5 and right=15. Queue = [5,15]. Step 3: Dequeue 5, print 5. Enqueue left=2 and right=7. Queue = [15,2,7]. Step 4: Dequeue 15, print 15. Enqueue left=null and right=null. Queue = [2,7]. Step 5: Dequeue 2, print 2. Enqueue left=null and right=null. Queue = [7]. Step 6: Dequeue 7, print 7. Enqueue left=null and right=null. Queue empty. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Enqueue and Dequeue Operations on a Queue
ladders.desc: Use a queue data structure to add elements to the back and remove from the front.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a queue, a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure, to manage elements. In the context of BFS for tree traversal, a queue is used to store nodes waiting to be processed. The intuition is that by enqueuing nodes as they are discovered and dequeuing them in the order they were added, we achieve level-order traversal. Enqueue adds an element to the back of the queue, and dequeue removes an element from the front. For example, using a list in Python, append() enqueues to the back, and pop(0) dequeues from the front. The step-by-step process: Initialize an empty queue. To enqueue an item, add it to the end. To dequeue, remove and return the first item. This ensures that nodes are processed in the order they were encountered, which is crucial for BFS. Time complexity for enqueue and dequeue is O(1) on average for efficient implementations, but pop(0) from a list is O(n) due to shifting; in practice, collections.deque is used for O(1) operations. Space complexity is O(n) for storing elements. This connects to L3 as the mechanism that enables BFS traversal. Common mistakes include using a stack (LIFO) instead of a queue, or incorrect indexing when implementing dequeue. Real code pattern: queue = [] to initialize, queue.append(item) to enqueue, item = queue.pop(0) to dequeue.

examples.input: Enqueue 1, then 2, then 3. Then dequeue twice.
examples.output: After enqueues: queue = [1,2,3]. After first dequeue: returns 1, queue = [2,3]. After second dequeue: returns 2, queue = [3].
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty queue []. Step 2: Enqueue 1: queue = [1]. Step 3: Enqueue 2: queue = [1,2]. Step 4: Enqueue 3: queue = [1,2,3]. Step 5: Dequeue: remove first element 1, return 1, queue = [2,3]. Step 6: Dequeue: remove first element 2, return 2, queue = [3]. Done.

examples.input: Enqueue 10, dequeue, enqueue 20, enqueue 30, dequeue.
examples.output: After operations: first dequeue returns 10, second dequeue returns 20, queue = [30].
examples.trace: Step 1: Start empty []. Step 2: Enqueue 10: queue = [10]. Step 3: Dequeue: remove 10, return 10, queue = []. Step 4: Enqueue 20: queue = [20]. Step 5: Enqueue 30: queue = [20,30]. Step 6: Dequeue: remove 20, return 20, queue = [30]. Done.

testCases.expected: First dequeue returns 1, second returns 2, queue empty
First dequeue returns 100, second returns 200, queue = [300]
First dequeue returns 7, second returns 8, queue empty

testCases.input: Enqueue 0, enqueue 1, enqueue 2, dequeue, enqueue 3, dequeue.
testCases.expected: First dequeue returns 0, second returns 1, queue = [2,3]

testCases.input: Enqueue 10, enqueue 20, dequeue, enqueue 30, dequeue, dequeue.
testCases.expected: First dequeue returns 10, second returns 20, third returns 30, queue empty

testCases.input: Enqueue 50, dequeue, dequeue (on empty queue after first dequeue).
testCases.expected: First dequeue returns 50, second dequeue raises error or returns null (depending on implementation)

testCases.input: Enqueue 3, enqueue 6, enqueue 9, dequeue, dequeue, dequeue.
testCases.expected: Dequeues return 3, 6, 9 in order, queue empty

testCases.input: Enqueue 12, enqueue 24, dequeue, enqueue 36, dequeue, enqueue 48.
testCases.expected: First dequeue returns 12, second returns 24, queue = [36,48]

testCases.input: Enqueue 99, dequeue, enqueue 88, dequeue, enqueue 77, dequeue.
testCases.expected: Dequeues return 99, 88, 77 in order, queue empty

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Pointers
ladders.desc: Understand a binary tree node with left and right child pointers and null references.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces a binary tree node, which is the basic building block of a binary tree. Each node contains a value and two pointers: one to its left child and one to its right child. If a node has no left or right child, the pointer is set to null. The intuition is that a binary tree is a hierarchical structure where each node can have at most two children, and these pointers define the connections between nodes. For example, in Python, a node can be represented as a class with attributes val, left, and right. Understanding this structure is prerequisite for manipulating trees, such as swapping children during mirroring. The step-by-step explanation: A node is created with a value, and initially left and right are null. To add children, assign new nodes to left or right pointers. This allows traversal and modification of the tree. In the context of the full problem, nodes are processed to swap these pointers. Common mistakes include confusing pointers with values, or not initializing pointers to null. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. This connects to L4 as nodes are the elements stored in the queue during BFS.

examples.input: Create a node with value 1, then set left child to a node with value 2 and right child to a node with value 3.
examples.output: Node 1 has left pointer to node 2 and right pointer to node 3. Node 2 has left=null, right=null. Node 3 has left=null, right=null.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1 with val=1, left=None, right=None. Step 2: Create node2 with val=2, left=None, right=None. Step 3: Create node3 with val=3, left=None, right=None. Step 4: Set node1.left = node2. Step 5: Set node1.right = node3. Done.

examples.input: Create a node with value 10, set left child to node with value 5 (which has left child value 2), and right child to null.
examples.output: Node 10 has left pointer to node 5, right=null. Node 5 has left pointer to node 2, right=null. Node 2 has left=null, right=null.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node10 with val=10, left=None, right=None. Step 2: Create node5 with val=5, left=None, right=None. Step 3: Create node2 with val=2, left=None, right=None. Step 4: Set node5.left = node2. Step 5: Set node10.left = node5. Done.

Node with value 1, left child value 2, right child null

testCases.expected: val=1, left=null, right points to node with val=3 (left=null, right=null)

testCases.expected: val=1, left points to node val=2 (left=null, right=null), right points to node val=3 (left=null, right=null)

approaches.name: Approach 4: Iterative DFS Using Stack
approaches.summary: Use depth-first search with an explicit stack to simulate recursion. Process nodes by pushing children onto stack after swapping left and right pointers, mimicking either pre-order or post-order traversal iteratively.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is tree height (worst O(n) for skewed tree)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Mirror Binary Tree Using Iterative DFS Stack
ladders.desc: Complete iterative solution using stack to mirror entire binary tree by swapping left/right children.
ladders.explanation: This approach mirrors a binary tree using an explicit stack to simulate depth-first traversal without recursion. The intuition is that mirroring requires visiting each node and swapping its left and right children. By using a stack, we can process nodes in a controlled order while maintaining the tree structure. The algorithm starts by pushing the root onto the stack if it exists. While the stack isn't empty, we pop a node, swap its left and right children, then push the children onto the stack if they exist. This mimics a pre-order traversal (process node, then push children) but works equally well as post-order since swapping doesn't depend on child processing order. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes, as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is tree height, storing at most h nodes on the stack (worst O(n) for skewed tree). Connection to L1: This full solution calls the stack processing logic repeatedly. Common mistakes include forgetting to check for null root, pushing children before swapping, or using queue instead of stack (which would do BFS). Real code pattern: Use while loop with stack, swap using temp variable.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3] (root=1, left=2, right=3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack = [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, swap left=2 and right=3 → left=3, right=2. Push children: stack = [3,2]. Step 3: Pop 3, swap left=None and right=None (no change). Push nothing. Step 4: Pop 2, swap left=None and right=None. Stack empty. Done.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3,4,5] (root=1, left=2 with left=4, right=3 with right=5)
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, swap left=2 and right=3 → left=3, right=2. Push 3 and 2 → stack = [3,2]. Step 3: Pop 3, swap left=None and right=5 → left=5, right=None. Push 5 → stack = [2,5]. Step 4: Pop 2, swap left=4 and right=None → left=None, right=4. Push 4 → stack = [5,4]. Step 5: Pop 5, swap left=None and right=None. Step 6: Pop 4, swap left=None and right=None. Stack empty.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Process Node and Push Children Using Stack
ladders.desc: Core function that pops node from stack, swaps children, and pushes valid children back.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the iterative DFS approach. The intuition is that for each node, we need to swap its left and right children, then continue processing the children. By using a stack, we maintain LIFO order to simulate depth-first traversal. The algorithm: 1) Pop node from stack. 2) Swap node.left and node.right using temporary variable. 3) If node.left exists, push onto stack. 4) If node.right exists, push onto stack. This ensures all nodes get processed exactly once. Time complexity for this operation is O(1) per node, but overall O(n). Space complexity contributes to O(h) stack usage. Connection to L0: This is repeated in the main loop. Connection to L2: This implements the swap-and-continue logic. Common mistakes include pushing children before swapping (which would process original structure), or forgetting null checks. Real code pattern: while stack: node = stack.pop(); swap; if left: stack.append(left); if right: stack.append(right).

examples.input: Node with value=1, left=2, right=3, stack initially empty
examples.trace: Step 1: Pop node 1 (from external context). Step 2: Swap left=2 and right=3 → left=3, right=2. Step 3: Push left=3 onto stack. Step 4: Push right=2 onto stack. Result: node swapped, stack = [3,2].

examples.input: Node with value=4, left=null, right=5, stack initially empty
examples.trace: Step 1: Pop node 4. Step 2: Swap left=null and right=5 → left=5, right=null. Step 3: Push left=5 onto stack. Step 4: Right is null, skip push. Result: node swapped, stack = [5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Swap Left and Right Children of a Node
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of exchanging left and right pointers of a binary tree node.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique for mirroring: swapping the left and right children of each node. The intuition is that mirroring a binary tree means creating a mirror image where left becomes right and right becomes left at every node. By swapping children recursively or iteratively, we transform the entire tree. The algorithm is simple: given a node, store node.left in temp, set node.left = node.right, then set node.right = temp. This takes O(1) time and O(1) space. Connection to L1: This swap is performed inside the node processing routine. Connection to L3: This assumes you can access node.left and node.right. Common mistakes include losing reference during swap (if done without temp), or swapping values instead of pointers (which would break tree structure). Real code pattern: temp = node.left; node.left = node.right; node.right = temp.

examples.input: Node with left=A, right=B
examples.trace: Step 1: temp = A. Step 2: node.left = B. Step 3: node.right = A. Result: left=B, right=A.

examples.input: Node with left=X, right=null
examples.trace: Step 1: temp = X. Step 2: node.left = null. Step 3: node.right = X. Result: left=null, right=X.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Tree Traversal Using Stack
ladders.desc: Traverse binary tree nodes using explicit stack instead of recursion.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for the iterative approach. The intuition is that recursion uses an implicit call stack, so we can simulate it with an explicit stack data structure. For DFS, we push nodes onto stack and process them in LIFO order. The algorithm: initialize stack with root. While stack not empty: pop node, process it (e.g., print value), push right child then left child (for pre-order). Time complexity is O(n) to visit all nodes. Space complexity is O(h) for stack depth. Connection to L2: This traversal framework can be adapted to include swapping. Connection to L4: This uses stack operations. Common mistakes include pushing null nodes, infinite loops from incorrect push order, or using queue for DFS. Real code pattern: stack = [root]; while stack: node = stack.pop(); process(node); if node.right: stack.append(right); if node.left: stack.append(left).

examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [1]. Step 2: pop 1, process 1. Push right=3, left=2 → stack = [3,2]. Step 3: pop 2, process 2. No children. Step 4: pop 3, process 3. Done.

Step 1: stack = [1]. Step 2: pop 1, process 1. Push 3,2 → stack = [3,2]. Step 3: pop 2, process 2. Push 4 → stack = [3,4]. Step 4: pop 4, process 4. Step 5: pop 3, process 3. Push 5 → stack = [5]. Step 6: pop 5, process 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop
ladders.desc: Fundamental stack operations: add to top (push) and remove from top (pop).
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental data structure operation used in the iterative approach. The intuition is that a stack follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) order, which mimics the behavior of recursion's call stack. Push adds an element to the top of the stack, and pop removes and returns the top element. In Python, list can be used as stack with append() for push and pop() for pop. Time complexity for both operations is O(1). Space complexity depends on number of elements stored. Connection to L3: These operations enable iterative traversal. Connection to L5: Stack is built on array/list concepts. Common mistakes include using wrong methods (e.g., insert(0) for push which is O(n)), or popping from empty stack. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(item); item = stack.pop().

examples.input: Push 1, push 2, pop, push 3, pop, pop
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = []. Step 2: push 1 → [1]. Step 3: push 2 → [1,2]. Step 4: pop → returns 2, stack = [1]. Step 5: push 3 → [1,3]. Step 6: pop → returns 3, stack = [1]. Step 7: pop → returns 1, stack = [].

examples.input: Push A, push B, push C, pop, pop, push D
examples.output: Pops: C, B; final stack has [A,D]
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = []. Step 2: push A → [A]. Step 3: push B → [A,B]. Step 4: push C → [A,B,C]. Step 5: pop → returns C, stack = [A,B]. Step 6: pop → returns B, stack = [A]. Step 7: push D → [A,D].

testCases.input: Push 12, push 13, push 14, pop, pop, pop

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure
ladders.desc: Basic building block: a node with value, left child, and right child pointers.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a binary tree node is. A binary tree node is a data structure that contains a value (e.g., integer) and two references or pointers to its left and right children, which can be other nodes or null. The intuition is that trees are built from these nodes linked together, forming a hierarchical structure. In code, this is typically a class with attributes: val, left, right. This foundation is crucial because mirroring operates on these pointers. Time and space for a single node are O(1). Connection to L4: Nodes are stored in stack. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with values, or not handling null children properly. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right.

examples.input: Create node with value=5, left=null, right=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Define node object. Step 2: Set val = 5. Step 3: Set left = None. Step 4: Set right = None. Result: node with value 5 and no children.

examples.input: Create node with value=10, left=node with value=20, right=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Create child node: val=20, left=None, right=None. Step 2: Create parent node: val=10, left=child node, right=None. Result: parent points to left child.

testCases.input: Node value=20, left=Node(21, Node(22)), right=Node(23, null, Node(24))
testCases.expected: Node(20, Node(21, Node(22)), Node(23, null, Node(24)))
